topic: Aspects of Political Life in the German-speaking World
subject: german

items.q: When does 'in' take the accusative vs dative?
items.correct: Accusative for movement INTO a place; dative for location IN a place.
items.misconception: It always takes the same case.
items.random_wrong: It depends on the speaker's mood.
items.why: Students don't realise two-way prepositions exist. Wechselpräpositionen change case by meaning: 'Ich gehe in die Stadt' (motion, accusative); 'Ich bin in der Stadt' (location, dative).
Spelled identically to English 'bald' but means SOON. 'Bis bald!' = 'See you soon!'. The English 'bald' translates as 'kahl' or 'glatzköpfig'. Pure faux ami.

items.q: How do you say 'I will drink coffee tomorrow' in German?
items.why: Students translate 'will' as 'will'. The German 'will' (from wollen) means 'want', not future. Future = WERDEN + infinitive. Also, German often uses present + time word ('Ich trinke morgen Kaffee') for near future.

items.q: Where does the verb go after 'weil'?
items.why: Correct answer: end of the clause. “second position” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: 16. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Grundgesetz adopted?
items.why: Exact answer: 1949. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
